story:
Three Weird Sisters—Tiana, Ariel, and Jasmine—are midway through “Double, double” when Jasmine’s sneeze shreds the rhythm and probably the spell. They banter about apothecaries, Jasmine’s rattly lungs, and past incantations gone sideways (see: Banquo and the questionable baby name “Fleance”). “Hopefully nothing too serious,” Tiana says—right before everything goes seriously sideways.

Cut to King Duncan’s banquet, celebrating his thugs for “repelling” an Irish threat largely inflated by the royal fool’s pub tales. In truth, the Scots had roughed up their own countryside, the Norse drifted in after the mess, and everyone split spoils to avoid real war. One hitch: Macdonwald, a despised snitch, threatened to spill everything. Macbeth and Banquo arranged for him not to be a problem—so when the banquet air curdles, candles blow out, and chains clank, the ghost that floats in is exactly who you think it is.

Macdonwald’s specter, reeking of rot and payback, greets the hall. He’s “on his way to Saint Peter” (if you’ll buy that) but drops one last truth-bomb first: he leans to Macbeth and whispers that Lady Macbeth has been intimate with both Duncan and Banquo. The air clears, the candles relight, and the head table turns into a powder keg. Macbeth rounds on Banquo—best friend, betrayer, the works. Banquo shrugs off the blast, claiming Lady Macbeth did the pursuing. Macbeth turns to Duncan, demanding why a king would take a loyal man’s wife. Duncan, smug as a cat in cream, implies he doesn’t lack options and isn’t exactly sorry.

Then Lady Macbeth takes center stage. She reminds Macbeth that his own roaming started this fire—he brought home more than stories from campaign season. At the apothecary, she ran into the Weird Sisters. Between their visions and the telltale evidence, she decided her husband was reckless, unfaithful, and far too dim to make her queen. If she wanted a crown, she’d pull the strings herself. She even presents a quiet revelation—a young servant boy with a name fit for Verona—strongly suggesting Macbeth’s secret son. The room is hers now; at her command, the hall empties in a heartbeat, leaving only Lady Macbeth, Macbeth, Banquo, and Duncan. Banquo, dismissed with a knowing touch, exits.

With the audience gone, Lady Macbeth openly aligns with Duncan, making her choice as public as a coronation. Macbeth, stunned and furious, reaches for a blade—too slow. She’s faster. With a jeweled pin-turned-dagger, she slashes his throat before he can act. No gory spectacle; just a clean, decisive end to the man who couldn’t keep his vows, his temper, or his ambitions in order.

As Macbeth fades, Lady Macbeth kneels and names the dominoes: Jasmine at the apothecary for a lung poultice, Ariel with the same unlucky ailment, the sisters’ “double, double” botched by a sneeze. Maybe that misfire opened the door for ghosts and truth to crash the party; maybe fate simply prefers a loud entrance. Either way, the secrets spilled, and she stayed on her chosen path.

She strips off her blood-stained gown—the Duncan plaid ruined—and stands unflinching. Duncan, impressed and undisguised in his approval, hails her: “Approach your king, my queen.” Whether this is triumph or the start of a darker spell, the Weird Sisters’ breeze has blown the court into a new, perilous weather.
